EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos show a large hardwood tree uprooted and fallen on a house. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved suddenly into the southern Poconos, Berks County, and the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 17, falling at a rate of over 1 inch per hour at times before ending very early Sunday morning, February 18.  Snowfall reports of 6 to 8 inches were common across the area.  Some of these higher reports spilled over into the northern portions of Bucks and Montgomery counties as well. This snow event shocked peoples' senses given that very warm above-normal temperatures preceded it the day before. Precipitation actually began as rain in and around Philadelphia which limited snowfall totals in those locations.  The wet and heavy nature of the snowpack from this storm made shoveling more difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 16th, then ended during the very early morning hours on Sunday, February 17th. Given the bulk of this snow fell on a Saturday evening, and the fact that antecedent ground temperatures were above freezing, societal impact from this event was minimal. Some representative snowfall totals include: 8.0 inches in Walnutport, 7.5 inches in Hellertown, 6.5 inches in Bethlehem, and 4.0 inches in Berlinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure extending from the Ohio Valley to the Piedmont of South Carolina consolidated off the Virginia Capes during the early morning of March 7th. This new primary low moved northeast and gradually deepened as it passed east of the Delaware and New Jersey Coasts on March 7th.||Precipitation gradually overspread the region during the overnight hours of March 6th to the 7th. Precipitation fell mainly as snow. ||The snow fell heavy at times. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches with localized amounts of 12 to 15 inches just north and west of I-95 in a swath from central Chester County northeast through central and southern Bucks County.  One emergency manager reported snowfall rates up to 4 inches per hour in thundersnow near Levittown (Bucks County). ||The snow contained large amounts of liquid, making it heavy and wet. This led to downed trees, limbs, and wires resulting in numerous power outages.||Governor Wolf declared a State of Emergency for all of eastern Pennsylvania for the event.||The City of Philadelphia declared a Snow Emergency beginning at 8 AM Wednesday, March 7th based on anticipated conditions. City and state offices were closed, as were all public and parochial schools. ||Montgomery County declared a Disaster Emergency from 7 PM Tuesday through 3 AM Thursday, March 8th.||A Declaration of Disaster Emergency was issued for Delaware County.  All government offices were closed.||Flights were cancelled at all the major airports due to the storm, and Amtrak cancelled at least some Wednesday service.||The heavy, wet snow, coupled with strong winds, caused power outages in addition to  existing power outages caused by the previous storm.  Hardest hit was Bucks County where up to 47,000 customers experienced power outages from this storm. In Montgomery County, approximately 12,000 customers lost power.  Up to 8,000 customers lost power in Delaware County from the storm. Monroe County experienced over 6,000 new power outages from this storm. Several hundred power outages were reported in Berks County.||The Port of Philadelphia was closed Wednesday, March 7th, and would reopen late the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Higher elevations throughout the county experienced higher snowfall totals from this event.  Western portions of the county, far from the center of the coastal low, received less than 2 inches from the event. Some snowfall reports include: 10.5 inches in Huffs Church (elevation of 1000 feet), 9.1 inches near Fleetwood, 8.8 inches in Earl Township, 7.4 inches in Boyertown, 3.9 inches northwest of Reading, 2.5 inches near Kutztown, 2.1 inches southwest of Reading, 1.9 inches in Hamburg, and 1.5 inches in Wernersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite high temperatures in the 50's and 60's across the region on April 1st, a cold front moving though the area during the morning gradually brought in colder air, which moved into the region by April 2nd. Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall.

A report of 9.0 inches of snow was received from Tobyhanna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across the region on March 1st. Meanwhile, a wave of low pressure developed along this front in the Ohio Valley and move eastward,  explosively deepening just southeast of Long Island on March 2nd. This large and very deep area of low pressure moved slowly east over the open waters of the North Atlantic Ocean through Sunday March 4th. This led to a variety of weather hazards during this time frame.||Strong northwest winds with gusts to near 60 mph occurred on March 2nd and 3rd. This led to widespread damage to trees and power lines, which in turn induced extensive power outages across the region. Southeast of Interstate 95, up to around 3 inches of snowfall was observed. To the northwest of Interstate 95, nearly 6 inches of snow was measured with localized amounts of around 9 inches. Higher elevations mainly to the north of Interstate 80 generally reported greater than 9 inches of snow, with amounts up to 24 inches above 2,000 feet.||On Friday night, March 2nd, Governor Tom Wolf activated the Pennsylvania National Guard to support local response to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy, wet snow accumulated to a depth of up to one-half foot feet throughout the county.  Representative snowfall reports include 6.0 inches in Hellertown, 5.6 inches in Bethlehem, 5.5 inches in Cherry Hill, and 3.2 inches in Martins Creek.  Wind gusts up to 60 MPH were common (a trained spotter at Bushkill Center reported a gust of 60 MPH at 1152EST on March 2nd) causing whiteout conditions during the afternoon and evening commute on March 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved from northeast to southwest across the area late Saturday afternoon April 15th into Sunday morning April 16th. Not only was there a significant drop in temperature coinciding with the passage of the front, but also a stark difference between high temperatures on Saturday and Sunday. On Saturday, high temperatures ranged from the mid-70s to mid-80s, but only made it to the mid-30s to mid-40s on Sunday. In general, there was a 40 degree temperature drop from Saturday to Sunday! ||The backdoor front became stationary south of the region then moved northward as a warm front Sunday night into Monday morning. Meanwhile, a strong cold front approached from the west and swept across the area Monday afternoon and evening, along with an attendant area of low pressure. This robust storm system tapped large amounts of moisture, which was lifted along the warm front as overrunning precipitation, and along the cold front as a line of showers and thunderstorms. ||As a result, heavy rainfall occurred across the region. A widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred, with localized amounts over 4 inches. This lead to flooding across portions of the area. Meanwhile, it was cold enough in the higher elevations of the Southern Poconos of Northeast Pennsylvania for freezing rain on Sunday. In particular, the Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS recorded 0.19 inches of ice accretion, and there was tree damage and power outages reported in Tobyhanna Pennsylvania.||Along the coast, an extended period of onshore flow occurred, beginning with east-northeast winds Saturday Night, which then shifted to the southeast on Sunday Night into Monday Morning April 16th. By Monday morning and afternoon, tidal flooding made its way up the Delaware River to Philadelphia as a result of the onshore flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Neshaminy Creek At Langhorne rose above flood stage of 9.0 feet. The maximum height was 10.29 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite high temperatures in the 50's and 60's across the region on April 1st, a cold front moving though the area during the morning gradually brought in colder air, which moved into the region by April 2nd. Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall.

Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall. Snowfall amounts of up to 2 inches occurred. A member of the public in Montgomeryville reported 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th. A NWS employee reported a few trees down in Northampton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved suddenly into the southern Poconos, Berks County, and the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 17, falling at a rate of over 1 inch per hour at times before ending very early Sunday morning, February 18.  Snowfall reports of 6 to 8 inches were common across the area.  Some of these higher reports spilled over into the northern portions of Bucks and Montgomery counties as well. This snow event shocked peoples' senses given that very warm above-normal temperatures preceded it the day before. Precipitation actually began as rain in and around Philadelphia which limited snowfall totals in those locations.  The wet and heavy nature of the snowpack from this storm made shoveling more difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 16th, then ended during the very early morning hours on Sunday, February 17th. Given the bulk of this snow fell on a Saturday evening, and the fact that antecedent ground temperatures were above freezing, societal impact from this event was minimal. Some representative snowfall totals include: 3.8 inches in Bartonsville, 3.5 inches in Pocono Summit, and 3.0 inches in Canadensis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved from northeast to southwest across the area late Saturday afternoon April 15th into Sunday morning April 16th. Not only was there a significant drop in temperature coinciding with the passage of the front, but also a stark difference between high temperatures on Saturday and Sunday. On Saturday, high temperatures ranged from the mid-70s to mid-80s, but only made it to the mid-30s to mid-40s on Sunday. In general, there was a 40 degree temperature drop from Saturday to Sunday! ||The backdoor front became stationary south of the region then moved northward as a warm front Sunday night into Monday morning. Meanwhile, a strong cold front approached from the west and swept across the area Monday afternoon and evening, along with an attendant area of low pressure. This robust storm system tapped large amounts of moisture, which was lifted along the warm front as overrunning precipitation, and along the cold front as a line of showers and thunderstorms. ||As a result, heavy rainfall occurred across the region. A widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred, with localized amounts over 4 inches. This lead to flooding across portions of the area. Meanwhile, it was cold enough in the higher elevations of the Southern Poconos of Northeast Pennsylvania for freezing rain on Sunday. In particular, the Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS recorded 0.19 inches of ice accretion, and there was tree damage and power outages reported in Tobyhanna Pennsylvania.||Along the coast, an extended period of onshore flow occurred, beginning with east-northeast winds Saturday Night, which then shifted to the southeast on Sunday Night into Monday Morning April 16th. By Monday morning and afternoon, tidal flooding made its way up the Delaware River to Philadelphia as a result of the onshore flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescues took place in the vicinity of Cobbs Creek.  A number of small streams and creeks left their banks, including Cobbs Creek and Frankford Creek.  The gauge on Cobbs Creek reached 7.09 feet, exceeding its flood stage of 7 feet.  The gauge on Frankford Creek reached 7.89 feet, exceeding its flood stage of 7 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy, wet snow accumulated to a depth of up to 10 inches under a deformation zone that established itself over the area during the storm.  9.5 inches of snow was reported in Wayne, 9.0 inches in Villanova, 7.0 inches in Newtown Square, 6.0 inches in Media, and 4.3 inches in Springfield.  A wind gust of 59 MPH was reported at Philadelphia International Airport at 2224EST on March 2nd, and a wind gust of 56 MPH was reported in Newtown Square at 1921EST.  Blowing and drifting snow made travel hazardous Friday afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved from northeast to southwest across the area late Saturday afternoon April 15th into Sunday morning April 16th. Not only was there a significant drop in temperature coinciding with the passage of the front, but also a stark difference between high temperatures on Saturday and Sunday. On Saturday, high temperatures ranged from the mid-70s to mid-80s, but only made it to the mid-30s to mid-40s on Sunday. In general, there was a 40 degree temperature drop from Saturday to Sunday! ||The backdoor front became stationary south of the region then moved northward as a warm front Sunday night into Monday morning. Meanwhile, a strong cold front approached from the west and swept across the area Monday afternoon and evening, along with an attendant area of low pressure. This robust storm system tapped large amounts of moisture, which was lifted along the warm front as overrunning precipitation, and along the cold front as a line of showers and thunderstorms. ||As a result, heavy rainfall occurred across the region. A widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred, with localized amounts over 4 inches. This lead to flooding across portions of the area. Meanwhile, it was cold enough in the higher elevations of the Southern Poconos of Northeast Pennsylvania for freezing rain on Sunday. In particular, the Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS recorded 0.19 inches of ice accretion, and there was tree damage and power outages reported in Tobyhanna Pennsylvania.||Along the coast, an extended period of onshore flow occurred, beginning with east-northeast winds Saturday Night, which then shifted to the southeast on Sunday Night into Monday Morning April 16th. By Monday morning and afternoon, tidal flooding made its way up the Delaware River to Philadelphia as a result of the onshore flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pocono Mountains Airport recorded 0.29 inches of radial ice accretion due to freezing rain. Radial conversion (0.90 planar accretion). In Tobyhanna, tree damage and multiple power outages were reported due to the ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved suddenly into the southern Poconos, Berks County, and the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 17, falling at a rate of over 1 inch per hour at times before ending very early Sunday morning, February 18.  Snowfall reports of 6 to 8 inches were common across the area.  Some of these higher reports spilled over into the northern portions of Bucks and Montgomery counties as well. This snow event shocked peoples' senses given that very warm above-normal temperatures preceded it the day before. Precipitation actually began as rain in and around Philadelphia which limited snowfall totals in those locations.  The wet and heavy nature of the snowpack from this storm made shoveling more difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 16th, then ended during the very early morning hours on Sunday, February 17th. Given the bulk of this snow fell on a Saturday evening, and the fact that antecedent ground temperatures were above freezing, societal impact from this event was minimal. Some representative snowfall totals include: 8.3 inches in Allentown, 7.6 inches in Salisbury Township, 7.0 inches in Center Valley, 6.5 inches in Alburtis, 6.5 inches at Lehigh Valley International Airport, 6.0 inches in East Texas, and 4.0 inches in New Smithville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked up the east coast interacting with a cold front which lead to rapid development of a winter storm across the state. This storm quickly moved out by the 5th. However, snowfall accumulations and gusty winds occurred with the storm. Top wind gusts were generally around 45 mph in the afternoon hours. Northwest of Philadelphia only a few inches of snow fell, with amounts around 5 inches closer to the Delaware River near Philadelphia. Numerous flights were cancelled. For the next week or so, the region felt impacts from severe cold and code blues were issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 4 to 6 across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of storms developed across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours of August 7, 2018 in a high CAPE environment as a weak shortwave crossed New England. Several reports of wind damage were received, primarily across the middle and lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down multiple trees in Millerstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure extending from the Ohio Valley to the Piedmont of South Carolina consolidated off the Virginia Capes during the early morning of March 7th. This new primary low moved northeast and gradually deepened as it passed east of the Delaware and New Jersey Coasts on March 7th.||Precipitation gradually overspread the region during the overnight hours of March 6th to the 7th. Precipitation fell mainly as snow. ||The snow fell heavy at times. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches with localized amounts of 12 to 15 inches just north and west of I-95 in a swath from central Chester County northeast through central and southern Bucks County.  One emergency manager reported snowfall rates up to 4 inches per hour in thundersnow near Levittown (Bucks County). ||The snow contained large amounts of liquid, making it heavy and wet. This led to downed trees, limbs, and wires resulting in numerous power outages.||Governor Wolf declared a State of Emergency for all of eastern Pennsylvania for the event.||The City of Philadelphia declared a Snow Emergency beginning at 8 AM Wednesday, March 7th based on anticipated conditions. City and state offices were closed, as were all public and parochial schools. ||Montgomery County declared a Disaster Emergency from 7 PM Tuesday through 3 AM Thursday, March 8th.||A Declaration of Disaster Emergency was issued for Delaware County.  All government offices were closed.||Flights were cancelled at all the major airports due to the storm, and Amtrak cancelled at least some Wednesday service.||The heavy, wet snow, coupled with strong winds, caused power outages in addition to  existing power outages caused by the previous storm.  Hardest hit was Bucks County where up to 47,000 customers experienced power outages from this storm. In Montgomery County, approximately 12,000 customers lost power.  Up to 8,000 customers lost power in Delaware County from the storm. Monroe County experienced over 6,000 new power outages from this storm. Several hundred power outages were reported in Berks County.||The Port of Philadelphia was closed Wednesday, March 7th, and would reopen late the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Higher elevations throughout the county experienced higher snowfall totals in excess of 6 inches from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite high temperatures in the 50's and 60's across the region on April 1st, a cold front moving though the area during the morning gradually brought in colder air, which moved into the region by April 2nd. Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall.

Snowfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches. A COOP Observer near Perkasie reported 2.1 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th. The Mount Pocono ASOS precipitation accumulation sensor was damaged by windblown debris. The ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 225 PM on April 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure over the Middle Atlantic, which involved several individual centers, slowly consolidated off the Virginia Capes Tuesday Morning, March 20th into Wednesday March 20st along a frontal boundary. This primary low, the fourth nor'easter of the month, gradually moved northeast Wednesday Night, to a position southeast of the 40 North/70 West ���Benchmark��� coordinates on Thursday Morning. ||As precipitation moved northward into a colder air mass across the area on on Tuesday, March 20th, snow and sleet developed in Southeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours.||During Tuesday evening, a mixed bag of precipitation developed, with freezing rain, sleet, and snow, except rain closer to the coast. ||Freezing rain lead to ice accretion up to 0.20��� across portions of far Southeastern Pennsylvania, causing downed trees and limbs, which impacted power lines, and lead to power outages. ||After midnight, during the overnight hours of Tuesday into Wednesday, a changeover to snow occurred across the Lehigh Valley, while the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania remained dry. A gradual change over to snow occurred across the remainder of Eastern Pennsylvania through the mid-morning hours of Wednesday, March 21st. Meanwhile, snow began in the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania during the mid to late morning hours of Wednesday. ||Moderate to heavy snowfall developed, and gradually overspread Eastern Pennsylvania from Wednesday Morning into the evening. Snowfall rates, particularly outside of the higher elevations of Northeast Pennsylvania were around one inch per hour at times. While the snow ended Wednesday Evening in the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania, it gradually ended from west to east across the remainder of the region around midnight. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from less than 6 inches in the Southern Poconos of Eastern Pennsylvania to around 15 inches in the Lehigh Valley, then tapering down to 6 to 8 inches in far Southeast Pennsylvania.||The weight of the heavy, wet snow brought down trees, limbs, and power lines across portions of far Southeast Pennsylvania, which lead to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as a wet, heavy snow during the evening hours on March 20th. After a lull during the overnight hours, a drier snow began falling, heavy at times, during the afternoon and evening hours on March 21st.  The heaviest snow from this event fell in the southern half of the county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th. There were reports of numerous trees down in Berks County, including a Social Media report of a tree down on wires which lead to a fire in Unionville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved suddenly into the southern Poconos, Berks County, and the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 17, falling at a rate of over 1 inch per hour at times before ending very early Sunday morning, February 18.  Snowfall reports of 6 to 8 inches were common across the area.  Some of these higher reports spilled over into the northern portions of Bucks and Montgomery counties as well. This snow event shocked peoples' senses given that very warm above-normal temperatures preceded it the day before. Precipitation actually began as rain in and around Philadelphia which limited snowfall totals in those locations.  The wet and heavy nature of the snowpack from this storm made shoveling more difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 16th, then ended during the very early morning hours on Sunday, February 17th. In the eastern half of the county, precipitation began as rain which kept snowfall totals much lower.Given the bulk of this snow fell on a Saturday evening, and the fact that antecedent ground temperatures were above freezing, societal impact from this event was minimal. Some representative snowfall totals include: 1.9 inches in Atglen, 1.6 inches in Nottingham, and 1.4 inches near West Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved from northeast to southwest across the area late Saturday afternoon April 15th into Sunday morning April 16th. Not only was there a significant drop in temperature coinciding with the passage of the front, but also a stark difference between high temperatures on Saturday and Sunday. On Saturday, high temperatures ranged from the mid-70s to mid-80s, but only made it to the mid-30s to mid-40s on Sunday. In general, there was a 40 degree temperature drop from Saturday to Sunday! ||The backdoor front became stationary south of the region then moved northward as a warm front Sunday night into Monday morning. Meanwhile, a strong cold front approached from the west and swept across the area Monday afternoon and evening, along with an attendant area of low pressure. This robust storm system tapped large amounts of moisture, which was lifted along the warm front as overrunning precipitation, and along the cold front as a line of showers and thunderstorms. ||As a result, heavy rainfall occurred across the region. A widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred, with localized amounts over 4 inches. This lead to flooding across portions of the area. Meanwhile, it was cold enough in the higher elevations of the Southern Poconos of Northeast Pennsylvania for freezing rain on Sunday. In particular, the Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS recorded 0.19 inches of ice accretion, and there was tree damage and power outages reported in Tobyhanna Pennsylvania.||Along the coast, an extended period of onshore flow occurred, beginning with east-northeast winds Saturday Night, which then shifted to the southeast on Sunday Night into Monday Morning April 16th. By Monday morning and afternoon, tidal flooding made its way up the Delaware River to Philadelphia as a result of the onshore flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of small streams and creeks left their banks, including the Wissahickon Creek.  The gauge on the Wissahickon Creek peaked at 6.07 feet, exceeding its flood stage of 5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching surface trough combined with moderate instability to produce widespread severe weather across the Mid-Atlantic. The event was well forecast with most of the area being highlighted in a Slight Risk Category by the Storm Prediction Center. Warm surface temperatures and dew points in the mid 70s lead to relatively high instability developed with forecast soundings suggesting 2000 J/kg CAPE values. An initial line of storms first developed over east-central Pennsylvania, then continued through the afternoon into the evening before storms congealed into several multi-cell clusters with several severe storms over New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris was reported to be covering Plow Rd. in Robeson Township from strong thunderstorm winds. Time best estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure along an occluded front in the Tennessee Valley redeveloped off the North Carolina Coast on Monday Night March 12th and underwent rapid deepening as it moved northeast toward to the 40 North/70 West ���benchmark��� coordinates on the Tuesday Morning March 13th. This strong low and a weaker area of low pressure in the Great Lakes lead to the development of precipitation across the region on the afternoon of the 12th, which persisted into the morning of the 13th. The precipitation was mainly snow to the north of The Pennsylvania Turnpike. Further to the south, precipitation began as rain and gradually changed over to snow on Monday Night. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite high temperatures in the 50's and 60's across the region on April 1st, a cold front moving though the area during the morning gradually brought in colder air, which moved into the region by April 2nd. Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Central Plains on April 3rd, deepening as it moved into the Saint Lawrence Valley on April 3rd and to Prince Edward Island on April 4th, due to a significant contrast in air masses with Continental Polar air to the north and Maritime Tropical air to the south. This lead to a strong cold frontal passage across the region on April 4th. In the wake of this front, colder air moving into the area and a tight pressure gradient lead to widespread damaging west-northwest wind gusts in excess of 50 mph on April 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure over the Middle Atlantic, which involved several individual centers, slowly consolidated off the Virginia Capes Tuesday Morning, March 20th into Wednesday March 20st along a frontal boundary. This primary low, the fourth nor'easter of the month, gradually moved northeast Wednesday Night, to a position southeast of the 40 North/70 West ���Benchmark��� coordinates on Thursday Morning. ||As precipitation moved northward into a colder air mass across the area on on Tuesday, March 20th, snow and sleet developed in Southeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours.||During Tuesday evening, a mixed bag of precipitation developed, with freezing rain, sleet, and snow, except rain closer to the coast. ||Freezing rain lead to ice accretion up to 0.20��� across portions of far Southeastern Pennsylvania, causing downed trees and limbs, which impacted power lines, and lead to power outages. ||After midnight, during the overnight hours of Tuesday into Wednesday, a changeover to snow occurred across the Lehigh Valley, while the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania remained dry. A gradual change over to snow occurred across the remainder of Eastern Pennsylvania through the mid-morning hours of Wednesday, March 21st. Meanwhile, snow began in the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania during the mid to late morning hours of Wednesday. ||Moderate to heavy snowfall developed, and gradually overspread Eastern Pennsylvania from Wednesday Morning into the evening. Snowfall rates, particularly outside of the higher elevations of Northeast Pennsylvania were around one inch per hour at times. While the snow ended Wednesday Evening in the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania, it gradually ended from west to east across the remainder of the region around midnight. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from less than 6 inches in the Southern Poconos of Eastern Pennsylvania to around 15 inches in the Lehigh Valley, then tapering down to 6 to 8 inches in far Southeast Pennsylvania.||The weight of the heavy, wet snow brought down trees, limbs, and power lines across portions of far Southeast Pennsylvania, which lead to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as a rain-snow-sleet-freezing rain mixture during the late afternoon hours on March 20th, adversely affecting the evening commute. After a lull during the overnight hours, a drier snow began falling by late morning on the 21st, becoming heavy at times during the afternoon and evening hours.  Some reported snowfall amounts include: 10.0 inches in Wayne, 9.6 inches in Chadds Ford, 9.0 inches in Garnet Valley, 8.7 inches in Ridley Park, 8.2 inches in Thornton, 8.0 inches in Upper Darby Township, 8.0 inches in Broomall, 7.6 inches at Philadelphia International Airport,  and 6.9 inches in Media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into an unseasonably warm and humid airmass over northwestern Pennsylvania during the evening of September 21, 2018. Moderate shear and CAPE were observed, and the area was under an Enhanced risk from SPC. Farther to the east, a moist southeasterly flow maintained low clouds through the day and conditions were much more stable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree and wires along Davey Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon made landfall near the Mississippi-Alabama border on the 4th.  It slowly moved north through the Ohio Valley for the next week.  This left Pennsylvania in an extremely moist southerly flow with widespread showers and thunderstorms through the period.   This rather unorganized weather pattern combined with already saturated soils brought mainly minor flooding to the area over a long period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dike on the Castleman River was breached and several roadways were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clearfield County reported flooding on the 10th.  At 1215 pm a shelter was opened at the Mahaffey Fire Station, 958 Market Street in the Borough of Mahaffey. Approximately 12 residents from four homes have been evacuated to the shelter.  At 439pm Chester Hill has flooding from Florence Street to Decatur Street. Three households have evacuated. Fire Chief and Mayor are asking households from Florence Street to Grace Street to evacuate due to more than three feet of water on the street and the Moshannon Creek is still rising. Several residents have refused to evacuate. A plan has been made to make a temporary shelter at the Station 14 Fire Station and then transport individuals to the Philipsburg YMCA if needed.   At 125pm on the 11th a swift water rescue team was deployed to Chester Hill to evacuate a 93 year old women from her home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved from northeast to southwest across the area late Saturday afternoon April 15th into Sunday morning April 16th. Not only was there a significant drop in temperature coinciding with the passage of the front, but also a stark difference between high temperatures on Saturday and Sunday. On Saturday, high temperatures ranged from the mid-70s to mid-80s, but only made it to the mid-30s to mid-40s on Sunday. In general, there was a 40 degree temperature drop from Saturday to Sunday! ||The backdoor front became stationary south of the region then moved northward as a warm front Sunday night into Monday morning. Meanwhile, a strong cold front approached from the west and swept across the area Monday afternoon and evening, along with an attendant area of low pressure. This robust storm system tapped large amounts of moisture, which was lifted along the warm front as overrunning precipitation, and along the cold front as a line of showers and thunderstorms. ||As a result, heavy rainfall occurred across the region. A widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred, with localized amounts over 4 inches. This lead to flooding across portions of the area. Meanwhile, it was cold enough in the higher elevations of the Southern Poconos of Northeast Pennsylvania for freezing rain on Sunday. In particular, the Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS recorded 0.19 inches of ice accretion, and there was tree damage and power outages reported in Tobyhanna Pennsylvania.||Along the coast, an extended period of onshore flow occurred, beginning with east-northeast winds Saturday Night, which then shifted to the southeast on Sunday Night into Monday Morning April 16th. By Monday morning and afternoon, tidal flooding made its way up the Delaware River to Philadelphia as a result of the onshore flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries.  Flooding occurred along Christopher Columbus Boulevard from Spring Garden Street to below the Ben Franklin Bridge.  Peak tide was 9.47 feet MLLW at Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow off the Atlantic Ocean combined and a warm front west of the Appalachians lead to an overrunning regime across the region. Temperatures across Carbon and Monroe Counties were cold enough for light freezing rain during the daylight hours of February 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow off the Atlantic Ocean and a warm front west of the Appalachians lead to an overrunning regime across the region. Temperatures across Carbon County were cold enough for light freezing rain during the daylight hours of February 23rd. The ASOS at the Pocono Mountains Airport, NJ, reported 0.07 of freezing rain at 12:32 PM EST, February 23, 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved suddenly into the southern Poconos, Berks County, and the Lehigh Valley during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 17, falling at a rate of over 1 inch per hour at times before ending very early Sunday morning, February 18.  Snowfall reports of 6 to 8 inches were common across the area.  Some of these higher reports spilled over into the northern portions of Bucks and Montgomery counties as well. This snow event shocked peoples' senses given that very warm above-normal temperatures preceded it the day before. Precipitation actually began as rain in and around Philadelphia which limited snowfall totals in those locations.  The wet and heavy nature of the snowpack from this storm made shoveling more difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late afternoon hours on Saturday, February 16th, then ended during the very early morning hours on Sunday, February 17th. In the eastern half of the county, precipitation began as rain which kept snowfall totals much lower. Given the bulk of this snow fell on a Saturday evening, and the fact that antecedent ground temperatures were above freezing, societal impact from this event was minimal. Some representative snowfall totals include: 3.0 inches in Lansdale, 2.3 inches in Wynnewood, and 1.0 inches in Wyncote.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across the region on March 1st. Meanwhile, a wave of low pressure developed along this front in the Ohio Valley and move eastward,  explosively deepening just southeast of Long Island on March 2nd. This large and very deep area of low pressure moved slowly east over the open waters of the North Atlantic Ocean through Sunday March 4th. This led to a variety of weather hazards during this time frame.||Strong northwest winds with gusts to near 60 mph occurred on March 2nd and 3rd. This led to widespread damage to trees and power lines, which in turn induced extensive power outages across the region. There was one fatality in Upper Merion Township, Montgomery County, PA, when a tree fell on a vehicle. ||Heavy rainfall occurred over eastern Pennsylvania on March 1st and 2nd, with widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches. Areal and minor small stream flooding occurred.||As the rain changed to snow on the 2nd, localized heavy snowfall occurred, particularly over the higher elevations. Southeast of Interstate 95, up to around 3 inches of snowfall was observed. To the northwest of Interstate 95, nearly 6 inches of snow was measured with localized amounts of around 9 inches. Higher elevations mainly to the north of Interstate 80 generally reported greater than 9 inches of snow, with amounts up to 24 inches above 2,000 feet.||On Friday night, March 2nd, Governor Tom Wolf activated the Pennsylvania National Guard to support local response to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy, wet snow accumulated to a depth of up to one-half foot feet throughout the county.  Representative snowfall reports include 6.0 inches in Fogelsville, 4.1 inches in Salisbury Township, 3.7 inches at Lehigh Valley International Airport, and 0.7 inches near Neffs. Wind gusts up to 60 MPH were common (Lehigh Valley International Airport reported a gust of 60 MPH at 1720EST on March 2nd) causing whiteout conditions during the afternoon and evening commute on March 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a warm front in the Ohio Valley, and high pressure to the north providing a source of colder near-freezing air, especially over the higher terrain, lead to the development of freezing rain across the Southern Poconos. Ice accretion ranged from a Trace to 0.15 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The approach of a warm front in the Ohio Valley, and high pressure to the north providing a source of colder near-freezing air, especially over the higher terrain, lead to the development of freezing rain across the Southern Poconos. A trained spotter in Coolbaugh at an elevation of 1,965 feet reported an ice accretion of 0.15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved from northeast to southwest across the area late Saturday afternoon April 15th into Sunday morning April 16th. Not only was there a significant drop in temperature coinciding with the passage of the front, but also a stark difference between high temperatures on Saturday and Sunday. On Saturday, high temperatures ranged from the mid-70s to mid-80s, but only made it to the mid-30s to mid-40s on Sunday. In general, there was a 40 degree temperature drop from Saturday to Sunday! ||The backdoor front became stationary south of the region then moved northward as a warm front Sunday night into Monday morning. Meanwhile, a strong cold front approached from the west and swept across the area Monday afternoon and evening, along with an attendant area of low pressure. This robust storm system tapped large amounts of moisture, which was lifted along the warm front as overrunning precipitation, and along the cold front as a line of showers and thunderstorms. ||As a result, heavy rainfall occurred across the region. A widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred, with localized amounts over 4 inches. This lead to flooding across portions of the area. Meanwhile, it was cold enough in the higher elevations of the Southern Poconos of Northeast Pennsylvania for freezing rain on Sunday. In particular, the Pocono Mountains Airport ASOS recorded 0.19 inches of ice accretion, and there was tree damage and power outages reported in Tobyhanna Pennsylvania.||Along the coast, an extended period of onshore flow occurred, beginning with east-northeast winds Saturday Night, which then shifted to the southeast on Sunday Night into Monday Morning April 16th. By Monday morning and afternoon, tidal flooding made its way up the Delaware River to Philadelphia as a result of the onshore flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries.  Peak tide was 9.47 feet MLLW at Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across the region on March 1st. Meanwhile, a wave of low pressure developed along this front in the Ohio Valley and move eastward,  explosively deepening just southeast of Long Island on March 2nd. This large and very deep area of low pressure moved slowly east over the open waters of the North Atlantic Ocean through Sunday March 4th. This led to a variety of weather hazards during this time frame.||Strong northwest winds with gusts to near 60 mph occurred on March 2nd and 3rd. This led to widespread damage to trees and power lines, which in turn induced extensive power outages across the region. There was one fatality in Upper Merion Township, Montgomery County, PA, when a tree fell on a vehicle. ||Heavy rainfall occurred over eastern Pennsylvania on March 1st and 2nd, with widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches. Areal and minor small stream flooding occurred.||As the rain changed to snow on the 2nd, localized heavy snowfall occurred, particularly over the higher elevations. Southeast of Interstate 95, up to around 3 inches of snowfall was observed. To the northwest of Interstate 95, nearly 6 inches of snow was measured with localized amounts of around 9 inches. Higher elevations mainly to the north of Interstate 80 generally reported greater than 9 inches of snow, with amounts up to 24 inches above 2,000 feet.||On Friday night, March 2nd, Governor Tom Wolf activated the Pennsylvania National Guard to support local response to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was recorded by a DEOS (Delaware Environmental Observing System) station in West Grove at 1505EST on March 2nd. Two cars of an East Penn Railroad train were tossed off elevated tracks around midday Friday, March 2nd, near Wilson Mill Road in Lower Oxford Township.  A boxcar and flatbed car were left on their sides along an embankment and the locomotive was pulled partially down the decline. There were no injuries reported.

Many other areas received under 6 inches. Blowing and drifting snow made travel hazardous Friday afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite high temperatures in the 50's and 60's across the region on April 1st, a cold front moving though the area during the morning gradually brought in colder air, which moved into the region by April 2nd. Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite high temperatures in the 50's and 60's across the region on April 1st, a cold front moving though the area during the morning gradually brought in colder air, which moved into the region by April 2nd. Meanwhile, a weak wave of low pressure developed along this front, and tracked south of the area. To the north of this low and where temperatures were cold enough, snow accumulated, especially near the Pennsylvania Turnpike and points north. The snow began after midnight on April 2nd and continued into the mid-morning hours. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to around 8 inches north of the Interstate 78 corridor. South of here, 1 to 4 inch amounts were common to the Pennsylvania Turnpike with the exception of points west of Interstate 476, where 3 to 6 inches occurred. To the south of the Pennsylvania Turnpike and Interstate 195 corridor, amounts tapered down from 1 inch in a southerly direction, with Philadelphia Pennsylvania reporting a Trace of snowfall. A trained spotter in Neffs reported 4.9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure over the Middle Atlantic, which involved several individual centers, slowly consolidated off the Virginia Capes Tuesday Morning, March 20th into Wednesday March 20st along a frontal boundary. This primary low, the fourth nor'easter of the month, gradually moved northeast Wednesday Night, to a position southeast of the 40 North/70 West ���Benchmark��� coordinates on Thursday Morning. ||As precipitation moved northward into a colder air mass across the area on on Tuesday, March 20th, snow and sleet developed in Southeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours.||During Tuesday evening, a mixed bag of precipitation developed, with freezing rain, sleet, and snow, except rain closer to the coast. ||Freezing rain lead to ice accretion up to 0.20��� across portions of far Southeastern Pennsylvania, causing downed trees and limbs, which impacted power lines, and lead to power outages. ||After midnight, during the overnight hours of Tuesday into Wednesday, a changeover to snow occurred across the Lehigh Valley, while the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania remained dry. A gradual change over to snow occurred across the remainder of Eastern Pennsylvania through the mid-morning hours of Wednesday, March 21st. Meanwhile, snow began in the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania during the mid to late morning hours of Wednesday. ||Moderate to heavy snowfall developed, and gradually overspread Eastern Pennsylvania from Wednesday Morning into the evening. Snowfall rates, particularly outside of the higher elevations of Northeast Pennsylvania were around one inch per hour at times. While the snow ended Wednesday Evening in the higher elevations of Eastern Pennsylvania, it gradually ended from west to east across the remainder of the region around midnight. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from less than 6 inches in the Southern Poconos of Eastern Pennsylvania to around 15 inches in the Lehigh Valley, then tapering down to 6 to 8 inches in far Southeast Pennsylvania.||The weight of the heavy, wet snow brought down trees, limbs, and power lines across portions of far Southeast Pennsylvania, which lead to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as a wet, heavy snow during the evening hours on March 20th. After a lull during the overnight hours, a drier snow began falling, heavy at times, during the afternoon and evening hours on March 21st.  The highest snowfall totals from the event occurred along and south of I-78. Some reported snowfall amounts include: 14.5 inches in Williams Township, 13.9 inches in Hellertown, 12.1 inches in Easton, 11.0 inches in Delp, 10.0 inches in Cherry Hill, 8.5 inches in Portland, 8.0 inches in Tatamy, and 5.6 inches in Nazareth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak EF-0 tornado occurred on this day near Heidelberg Township in northern Lehigh County, Pennsylvania. A closed upper low centered over the Ohio Valley and a filling surface low rotating around the upper low near Lake Ontario helped initiate weak thunderstorms and heavy rainfall across east-central Pennsylvania. Enough instability allowed for several strong to severe thunderstorms to form across the Lehigh Valley on this afternoon in the wake of a strong and progressive coastal low that pivoted northward around the closed upper-low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado occurred on the evening of July 22, 2018 in |Heidelberg Township, PA. The well defined path through the corn|field and twisted nature of the tree damage and eye witness |accounts helped to determine this was tornadic damage. The tornado was brief and moved northwest for around 0.8 miles. Damage started with several small trees and limbs being downed along Church Road near the Heidelberg Church. Further west, areas of corn were blown over, with areas each being blown over in several different directions, near some solar panels at the Heidel Hollow Farm. There was also damage to sheet metal on the sides and roof of a barn at the farm. These areas of damage fell in a relatively straight line.

The right lane of northbound Route 61 was closed in Leesport in the area of Shackamaxon Street due to a downed utility pole. The tornado crossed Beam Church Rd and continued east|southeast into Gray. Several buildings sustained roof or siding|damage from downed trees. Many trees were completely uprooted|because of saturated soil with standing water underneath the root|ball. The tornado crossed W 3rd street where it flipped a car port|and moved a trailer about 6 feet. A large patch of shingles was|tossed over a house and landed on a car windshield.||Further east, several healthy trees were snapped 10-30 feet off the|ground consistent with winds of up to 90 mph. Healthy pines fell in|a convergent pattern perpendicular to storm motion. This pattern is|consistent with a tornado. The tornado appears to have lifted right|before a stream in the block bordered by W 3rd Street, E 1st Street,|Main St and Center St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a nearly stationary boundary that crept southeastward from the Great Lakes.  Precipitable water values of around 1.6 inches and weak steering flow along the boundary led to a few areas of training thunderstorms.  Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 3.0 inches on already saturated ground led to several areas of flash flooding in Washington, Allegheny, and Westmoreland Counties. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed south of a stalled frontal boundary that was draped from NW to SE across southwestern Pennsylvania. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a nearly stationary boundary that crept southeastward from the Great Lakes.  Precipitable water values of around 1.6 inches and weak steering flow along the boundary led to a few areas of training thunderstorms.  Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 3.0 inches on already saturated ground led to several areas of flash flooding in Washington, Allegheny, and Westmoreland Counties.  The worst flooding was near Herminie, where up to 14 homes were impacted and a mobile home was shifted off of its foundation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager reported that Happy Hollow Road was closed from Long Acre Drive to Scenery Drive due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe developed ahead of and along a passing cold front on the 4th. While instability was rather modest, shear supported some storms to severe levels, with damaging wind as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported two trees down in Sheakleyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary that was situated over the upper Ohio Valley. Strong to severe storms resulted in several reports of downed trees and hail in southwestern Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported dozens of trees snapped or uprooted. Roof barn and carport removed, along with other structural damage noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary that was situated over the upper Ohio Valley. Strong to severe storms resulted in several reports of downed trees and hail in southwestern Pennsylvania and eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported that high tension lines were down along Valley View Road. There is widespread tree damage in this vicinity and along Munntown Road associated with earlier reported downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front continued to linger in the vicinity of the Mason-Dixon Line.  A marked temperature contrast existed across the front, with values in the 50s over northwestern Pennsylvania and numbers well into the 80s over northern West Virginia. This boundary helped to produce more severe weather during the afternoon and evening hours.  Storms with hail larger than quarters and downed trees were reported, along with isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public citizen provided photos and a video of hail up to two inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell on the morning of May 27 from Bucks County in southeastern Pennsylvania eastward into central New Jersey.  Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were common with some locations receiving up to 5 or 6 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening hours of May 10, 2018. This front touched off several broken lines of convection, with storms producing both large hail and wind damage.  The largest storm produced ping pong ball-sized hail in southern York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down wires in Lewistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front continued to linger in the vicinity of the Mason-Dixon Line.  A marked temperature contrast existed across the front, with values in the 50s over northwestern Pennsylvania and numbers well into the 80s over northern West Virginia. This boundary helped to produce more severe weather during the afternoon and evening hours.  Storms with hail larger than quarters and downed trees were reported, along with isolated flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm tracked north from New Jersey to northern New York. This system triggered several rounds of heavy rain producing thunderstorms which caused severe flash flooding and major damages in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding was occurring around Troy Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pittsburgh region continued to sit under an upper level ridge while tropical moisture lingered in the Northeast U.S. Sufficient instability allowed for a few strong storms to develop, causing tree damage. Localized flooding was the main concern due to a static atmosphere with light flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported that vehicles were stuck in flooded roadway at Allegheny River Blvd and Sandy Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable airmass lead to thunderstorms in the northeast ahead of a cold front. PWAT values continued to be high; the PBZ 12Z sounding measured 2.04 inches (this was noted to be the top 5 reading for PWAT of all time). Localized flooding continued to be the concern as the region has already had a number of rainy days prior. A Flash Flood Watch was issued by the office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported that a Port Authority bus with passengers was taking on water and disable at Thompson Run Road. Swift water team dispatched at Thompson and Evergeen Road. Spring Garden Ave flooded at Wilson Road in Northview Heights; vehicle stranded. Residence were flooded at 11 Sedgwick Street. Six people were stranded on the 2nd floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed with the passage of a cold front. There was sufficient moisture, modest instability, and weak wind shear. Forecasted soundings predicted 0-3km lapse rates at an excess of 8.5 C/km. Mid-level dry air helped create an environment for strong downdrafts/damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported street and lot flooding along Chartiers Creek near Route 40/Interstate 70 intersection. Stagnant Water in photos appears to be around 1 foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the eastern Great Lakes region during the afternoon of the 3rd. This front sparked off a few thunderstorms one of which become severe in Lackawanna County in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical-like moisture lingered over the region as an upper level disturbance and slow moving surface front drifted into northern Pennsylvania. Thunderstorms continually developed over the area, producing significant amounts of rainfall in a short amount of time. This triggered areas of flash flooding in previously saturated, and flood damage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters were washing out portions of Route 267 near Lawton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was affecting a diner on route PA 267.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along an approaching cold front. Mostly clear skies and scattered cumulus clouds helped support surface heating and instability. Temperatures got up to the mid-80s; dewpoints were in the upper 60s. MLCAPE values neared 2000 J/kg; deep layer vertical wind shear increased in the afternoon hours.The Storm Prediction Center issued Severe Thunderstorm Watches for portions of Eastern Ohio and Western PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call center reported multiple trees down in Sandy Creek Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon came ashore along the central Gulf Coast on the night of September 4th.  The tropical cyclone dissipated over Missouri by the night of September 7th. Moisture from the decaying tropical system interacted with a surface low and frontal boundary, spreading widespread heavy rainfall across the region over a 3 day period and creating widespread flooding. 72 hour totals across southwest Pennsylvania from 2 inches to a little over 7 inches. Storm rainfall total for Pittsburgh ranks #2 for tropical remnants at 5.64 inches (Hurricane Ivan in 2004 still remains #1 at 5.94 inches).  Disaster declarations were made in Allegheny County (where five municipalities had already done so) and in Westmoreland County.||The U.S Small Business Administration awarded low-interest loans to qualifying victims of flooding. The money follows Governor Tom Wolf's request to SBA to declare a disaster in Westmoreland County. The disaster declaration also makes homeowners, renters, and businesses eligible for the loans in Washington, Fayette, Allegheny, and Indiana County. Low-interest loans of up to $200,000 are available to homeowners to repair or replace damaged or destroyed real estate, Wolf���s office said. Regulations permit loans of up to $40,000 to repair or replace personal property. Businesses and nonprofits can borrow up to $2 million to restore damaged or destroyed buildings, inventory, equipment and assets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call center reported that Ceylon Road was closed due to high water.

Moisture from the decaying tropical system interacted with a surface low and frontal boundary, spreading widespread heavy rainfall across the region over a 3 day period and creating widespread flooding. 72 hour totals across southwest Pennsylvania from 2 inches to a little over 7 inches. Storm rainfall total for Pittsburgh ranks #2 for tropical remnants at 5.64 inches (Hurricane Ivan in 2004 still remains #1 at 5.94 inches).  Disaster declarations were made in Allegheny County (where five municipalities had already done so) and in Westmoreland County.||The U.S Small Business Administration awarded low-interest loans to qualifying victims of flooding. The money follows Governor Tom Wolf's request to SBA to declare a disaster in Westmoreland County. The disaster declaration also makes homeowners, renters, and businesses eligible for the loans in Washington, Fayette, Allegheny, and Indiana County. Low-interest loans of up to $200,000 are available to homeowners to repair or replace damaged or destroyed real estate, Wolf���s office said. Regulations permit loans of up to $40,000 to repair or replace personal property. Businesses and nonprofits can borrow up to $2 million to restore damaged or destroyed buildings, inventory, equipment and assets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report that Montour Creek was out if its bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along an approaching cold front. Mostly clear skies and scattered cumulus clouds helped support surface heating and instability. Temperatures got up to the mid-80s; dewpoints were in the upper 60s. MLCAPE values neared 2000 J/kg; deep layer vertical wind shear increased in the afternoon hours.The Storm Prediction Center issued Severe Thunderstorm Watches for portions of Eastern Ohio and Western PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 Call Center reported tree down and blocking North Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south oriented stationary front extended along the spine of the Appalachian mountains through Pennsylvania and into the southern counties of New York state. Areas of significant flash flooding developed across Northeast Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed east of Cleveland from a breeze off of Lake Erie near 17Z. Areas south and east of the line gave way to more storms as outflow boundaries converged. With high PWAT values (near 1.90) flash flooding became an issue with training storms. |Wet microbursts were also a concern with DCAPE values near 500kjg-1. The Weather Prediction Center had our region under a slight risk for excessive rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported flooded intersection near Squaw Run Road/Field Club Road and at the intersection Fairview Road and Old Mill Road. An emergency manager also reported rushing water near Woodland Farms Road. Fox Chapel Stone Supply INC worker says the store took on 2 ft of water (WPXI reported). Aspinwall declared a state of emergency. The O'Hara Township manager reported that some homes may be total losses; said total infrastructure losses in the township added up to $1.3 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich tropical moisture remained across the Northeast region. Precipitable water values continued to be high at 1.90 to 2.00 inches. Morning soundings showed very little upper level flow. A static atmosphere prompted a risk for flooding; storms that developed tended to move very little. Isolated wet microbursts were a potential due to CAPE values over 2000 J/kg. Storms developed from outflow boundaries and light convergence aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported that a tree fell on a car in Uniontown Country Club. A number of other trees were reported down across Uniontown. East Fayette Street was closed for a period of time due to trees being down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity was in place across western Pennsylvania underneath a strong ridge, which allowed instability to build during the afternoon of June 18th.  Thunderstorms initially formed on a lake breeze boundary off of Lake Erie and dropped southeastward across areas north of Pittsburgh, resulting in a few wind damage events in Clarion and Jefferson Counties.  A second round of storms formed along a cold front and dropped in a similar fashion across western Pennsylvania, again resulting in some wind damage reports northeast of Pittsburgh.  The storms diurnally weakened thereafter, with the severe weather threat over by midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager reported a tree and wires were blown down onto Route 58 east of Eau Claire, with the road closed as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania the evening of June 13, 2018, with scattered storms developing in a high-shear, modest CAPE environment. Most of the storms that developed displayed rotation, and wind damage reports were received from a line of storms that crossed Tioga County and a single cell that crossed Cambria County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near Parkhurst Street in Blossburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in State College PA confirmed an EF1 tornado near Gallitzin in Cambria County, Pennsylvania on 06/13/2018. The tornado paralleled Syberton Road as it traveled east southeast and resulted in numerous snapped and uprooted trees along its path. The path length was approximately half-a-mile, with a maximum path width of 150 yards and a maximum wind speed of 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in State College PA has confirmed an EF1 tornado near Gallitzin in Cambria County Pennsylvania on 06/13/2018. The path length was approximately half-a-mile, with a path width of 150 yards and a maximum wind speed of 90 mph. The tornado paralleled Syberton Road as it traveled east southeast and resulted in numerous snapped and uprooted trees along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south across northern Ohio and northwest Pennsylvania on the evening of the 1st. The environment lacked shear and instability keeping the risk of severe weather low. However, the slow storm motions and residual warm moist air in the low levels made rainfall efficiencies high. During the afternoon some storms developed ahead of the approaching cold front, with rainfall rates estimated over 4��� per hour.  A slow moving cell formed near Kinsman extending to the dam at the Pymatuning Lake between 4 and 6 pm. Flash flooding resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Friday June 1, 2018  from 6:30 pm ��� 10:00 PM the Sugar Run in the Shenango River basin just south and downstream of the Pymatuning Dam flooded.  According to USGS records it was the second highest flow recorded at the Pymatuning Weir Gauge Station.  The highest flow was recorded on September 9, 2004 as a result of Hurricane Ivan.  However that high flow included input from the Pymatuning Dam.   During the June 1, 2018 event, the dam gates at 6:45 pm  before Sugar Run reached flood stage.   This event appears to be the worst flash flood on Sugar Run since the start of data collection in 1933.  In addition to the destruction of a park hiking bridge ($30,000 estimated replacement).  Residents in four private homes/cottages in the Douthette Allottment �� mile below the dam were evacuated and the structures received minor flood damage due to 4-8��� of water on the first floor.   A rain gauge at the Pymatuning Park Office recorded 1.66 inches of precipitation from as of 8 pm, however this gauge was sited north of the heaviest rainfall.   Anecdotal accounts reported 4-5 inches in rain gauges directly north east of Kinsman, OH in the watershed.  Doppler radar estimates of 1.5-3.5��� fell in the area, however radar estimates have a low bias due to the height of the radar beam at that location.

A second round of storms formed along a cold front and dropped in a similar fashion across western Pennsylvania, again resulting in some wind damage reports northeast of Pittsburgh.  The storms diurnally weakened thereafter, with the severe weather threat over by midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County 911 officials reported that a tree and utility lines were blown down, blocking Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeastward across Pennsylvania and New Jersey Sunday afternoon, causing a line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form across central and southeastern parts of Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado was confirmed by a NWS survey team to have touched down in east-central Dauphin County. The path length was approximately 1.6 miles, with a path width of 100 yards and a maximum wind speed of 100 mph. The tornado began at the intersection of Piketown Road and Appalachian Trail and ended 200 yards west of the Full Gospel Mission Church on Fishing Creek Valley Road. Hundreds of trees were snapped and uprooted along its path. The downed trees caused damage to the roofs of 4 homes, as well as several sheds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down near Piketown in Dauphin Count. The tornado had a path length of approximately 1.6 miles and a max path width of approximately 100 yards. Max winds were estimated around 100 mph.  Hundreds of trees were uprooted or snapped, and downed trees caused damage to the roofs of 4 homes and several sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 3rd.  The combination of dry air aloft and sufficient deep layer shear allowed for the generation of a few severe thunderstorms, which mainly caused tree damage.  One brief tornado was seen near Finleyville in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado was confirmed along Airport Road near Finleyville.  The tornado touched down just south of a series of hangars along the southern end of the Finleyville Airport runway.  It tracked southeast across a residential property and into a local valley before dissipating.  Several large pine trees suffered limb damage, with one mature pine snapped entirely at the base.  A two-story garage received damage to both entry doors as well as the removal of siding.  A wooden vehicle trailer was also lifted and displaced approximately 30 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense cold front move through central New York and northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 15th. This front produced a major squall line which eventually evolved into a derecho with estimated wind speeds of up to 115 mph in Wayne County, Pennsylvania. The derecho moved into Sullivan County, New York with winds just shy of 100 mph. Thousands of trees were snapped and uprooted along at least a 35 mile path with many homes damaged from falling trees and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles were blown off of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front associated with an area of developing low pressure over northern Illinois moved across northern Ohio and northwestern Pennsylvania late on October 1st through the morning hours of the 2nd.  This allowed strong southerly flow to bring abnormally warm and moist air into the area. Temperatures in the upper 70s to low 80s created moderate instability ahead of the front during the afternoon. Strong low-level wind shear was in place so the environment was primed for severe weather with tornadoes possible. The low pressure lifted northeast to southern Ontario by noon on the 2nd with the associated cold front moving eastward across Ohio. The main emphasis for the tornadic development was an outflow boundary from morning convection over the lake. This boundary (maybe the warm front) was oriented west to east across Ashtabula and Crawford counties. The first storms along this boundary initiated between 1:00 and 2:00 PM in Ashtabula and southern Erie County.  These thunderstorms eventually produced three tornadoes in Crawford County.  The strongest of the three struck a nursing home near Conneautville causing significant damage.  The other two tornadoes struck rural areas and resulted in mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video from social media showed several trees were blown down near the Fountain House Corners area at Highway 198 and Highway 86.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense cold front move through central New York and northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 15th. This front produced a major squall line which eventually evolved into a derecho with estimated wind speeds of up to 115 mph in Wayne County, Pennsylvania.
